key_insight: The paper introduces a modular calculus that separates optimizer geometry from nongeometric mechanisms and proves that full positive-definite geometry exactly captures strict descent directions away from critical points.
engineering_relevance: It provides a design-audit framework for adaptive optimizers, helping engineers reason about which modules are needed to achieve specific update directions under budget, state, and oracle constraints.
method: Defines geometric modules as positive cometrics and nongeometric modules for information, memory, control, operators, noise, targets, and discretization.
Proves a direction-expressivity theorem plus exact residual conditions for diagonal and block metric families.
Lifts pointwise residuals to trajectory-level residual complexity that accounts for geometry variation over time.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports diagnostic prototypes only: a full-metric probe solves deterministic quadratic benchmarks to numerical precision, and a Muon-style PyTorch candidate offers small-scale auditing evidence.
watch_item: The framework is primarily theoretical and benchmark-language oriented, so its practical value depends on careful implementation and may not translate directly into large-scale optimizer performance gains.
paper_title: Geometric--Nongeometric Optimizer Calculus: A Modular Language for Reachable Gradient Methods
